one_line_takeaway: IntentGuard reframes indirect prompt injection defense around tracing which instructions a reasoning LLM actually intends to execute (via structured 'thinking intervention' prompting) back to their trusted/untrusted origin, cutting adaptive PAIR-attack ASR from 100.0%/72.6% (vanilla) to 8.5%/10.9% on Mind2Web with near-zero utility loss and zero false positives across two backbones and two agentic benchmarks.
key_finding: On Mind2Web with Qwen3-32B, IntentGuard reduces ASR under the strongest adaptive PAIR attack from 100.0% (Vanilla) to 8.5%, while benign Utility drops only slightly from 0.840 to 0.810 (the paper's single noted case of utility degradation) and FPR is 0.000. On the same benchmark with gpt-oss-20B, PAIR ASR drops from 72.6% (Vanilla) to 10.9% (IntentGuard), with benign Utility essentially preserved (0.740 -> 0.745). On AgentDojo, IntentGuard preserves benign Utility exactly relative to Vanilla for both backbones (Qwen3-32B: 0.773 -> 0.773; gpt-oss-20B: 0.649 -> 0.649) with FPR 0.000, while reducing PAIR ASR from 0.723 -> 0.092 (Qwen3-32B) and 0.731 -> 0.104 (gpt-oss-20B); GCG ASR drops from 0.624 -> 0.039 (Qwen3-32B) and from 0.642 -> 0.049 (gpt-oss-20B). Against PromptArmor specifically on AgentDojo under PAIR, the paper reports IntentGuard achieves over 20% higher utility and more than 50% lower ASR (consistent with Table 1: IntentGuard 0.687 Util / 0.092 ASR vs. PromptArmor 0.432 Util / 0.659 ASR for Qwen3-32B). Ablations: combining start-of-thinking prefilling and end-of-thinking refinement drives ASR below 0.1, versus ~0.3 (prefilling alone) or ~0.2 (refinement alone); adversarial-reasoning in-context demonstration gives the strongest robustness among the three demonstration variants. Sparse vs. dense embedding matching for Origin Tracing perform comparably in utility/ASR (e.g., Qwen3-32B: sparse 0.687 Util/0.092 ASR/0.075s vs. dense 0.692 Util/0.094 ASR/0.274s) but sparse is markedly faster. Origin Tracing IoU stays in the 0.973-0.990 range across all tested window-ratio x threshold combinations, indicating low sensitivity to hyperparameters. The IIA faithfulness confusion matrix (Qwen3-32B, AgentDojo, PAIR attack) shows 83.8% of ground-truth instructions on the diagonal (correctly predicted intent-vs-execution outcome); of the remaining cases, 5.3% are attributed to the model intending but failing to correctly call the tool (not a true IIA failure per the authors), leaving what the authors characterize as a true 'unfaithful' IIA rate of 10.9%.
core_contribution: The paper introduces IntentGuard, a training-free defense against indirect prompt injection attacks (IPIAs) built on a new abstraction: an 'instruction-following intent analyzer' (IIA). The paper's central claim is that what matters for prompt injection is not whether malicious-looking text is present in untrusted data, but whether the LLM internally intends to follow it. IntentGuard operationalizes this in three steps: (1) Intent Extraction -- an IIA produces a structured list of instructions the model intends to follow; (2) Origin Tracing -- a sliding-window embedding-similarity search traces each intended instruction back to its source segment in the prompt (trusted vs. untrusted); (3) Injection Mitigation -- if an intended instruction traces back to an untrusted/data segment, the system either alerts the user for confirmation ('alert mode') or masks the suspicious region and reruns inference ('recovery mode'). To instantiate the IIA on reasoning-enabled LLMs, the authors propose three 'thinking intervention' techniques adapted from Wu et al. (2025) (cited as the closest related work, explicitly credited as the origin of the general thinking-intervention idea): (1) start-of-thinking prefilling -- prefilling the start of the reasoning chain with text that induces the model to list intended instructions; (2) end-of-thinking refinement -- replacing the first '</think>' token with a forced refinement step; (3) (adversarial) in-context demonstration -- prepending an example reasoning trace showing the desired structured-instruction-listing pattern, with three demonstration variants tested (format specification, conflict reasoning, adversarial reasoning).
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against LLM-powered agents. LLM input is modeled as a concatenation of segments (system prompt, user prompt, data/tool-response segments), each labeled trusted or untrusted; any instruction whose origin traces to an untrusted segment is treated as malicious. Attacker capability: can inject arbitrary malicious instructions into any untrusted segment, and is explicitly assumed white-box adaptive -- full knowledge of the defense algorithm, parameters, model architecture, and weights. Evaluated attack classes: (i) Template attacks (fixed AgentDojo injection templates), (ii) Beam Search attacks (Sadasivan et al. 2024), (iii) GCG attacks (gradient-based adversarial suffixes, Zou et al. 2023), and (iv) PAIR-adaptive attacks (Chao et al. 2025) -- an LLM-driven iterative refinement attacker that the authors extend with a custom prompt specifically instructed to keep the injected instruction covert during the model's stated reasoning/plan while still causing the target tool call to execute (i.e., an attack explicitly adapted against IntentGuard's own mechanism). For GCG/Beam Search, the paper also adapts the optimization target to two attack objectives against IntentGuard specifically (non-thinking elicitation, and manipulated thinking that omits the injected instruction from the stated plan), reporting the higher of the two ASRs.
limitations: The paper explicitly flags several limitations/future directions in its Discussion section: (1) the current IIA relies on prompting an off-the-shelf model via thinking intervention rather than fine-tuning, which the authors say could improve efficiency and reasoning faithfulness if pursued; (2) the IIA still has a nonzero 'unfaithful' rate (10.9% per the confusion-matrix analysis) where the model's actual execution doesn't match its stated intent; (3) the method as instantiated only applies to reasoning-enabled LLMs that verbalize a 'thinking' trace -- it is not shown to generalize to non-reasoning models; (4) origin tracing operates over text spans rather than input tokens, which the authors note limits straightforward extension to multi-modal models; (5) the approach depends on the model producing sufficiently faithful/parseable structured reasoning even under adversarial pressure -- the PAIR attack is explicitly designed to try to defeat this by keeping injected instructions out of the stated plan, and although ASR is greatly reduced it is not driven to zero (residual ASR of 8.5%-10.9% under PAIR on Mind2Web, 9.2%-10.9% under PAIR on AgentDojo); (6) the threat model assumes trust labels on input segments are correctly assigned by the system designer, and the paper acknowledges tasks that legitimately require following instructions in retrieved/untrusted data are handled only via the alert mode requiring explicit user labeling, not automatically resolved.

datasets_used: AgentDojo (97 tasks across 4 environments: workspace, communication, banking, travel; 629 adversarial test cases)
Mind2Web with AdvAgent attack suite (440 tasks across 4 domains)
quantitative_results: AgentDojo, Qwen3-32B, PAIR attack: ASR 0.723 (Vanilla) -> 0.092 (IntentGuard); Utility 0.464 (Vanilla) -> 0.687 (IntentGuard); benign Utility 0.773 for both
AgentDojo, gpt-oss-20B, PAIR attack: ASR 0.731 (Vanilla) -> 0.104 (IntentGuard); Utility 0.389 (Vanilla) -> 0.643 (IntentGuard); benign Utility 0.649 for both
Mind2Web, Qwen3-32B, PAIR attack: ASR 1.000 (Vanilla) -> 0.085 (IntentGuard); benign Utility 0.840 (Vanilla) -> 0.810 (IntentGuard, the one case with utility drop)
Mind2Web, gpt-oss-20B, PAIR attack: ASR 0.726 (Vanilla) -> 0.109 (IntentGuard); benign Utility 0.740 (Vanilla) -> 0.745 (IntentGuard)
IntentGuard FPR = 0.000 in all reported benign settings for both backbones/datasets
Ablation (Qwen3-32B, AgentDojo, PAIR): start-of-thinking prefilling alone -> ASR ~0.3; end-of-thinking refinement alone -> ASR ~0.2; both combined -> ASR < 0.1
Origin Tracing sparse vs. dense embeddings (Qwen3-32B): sparse Utility 0.687 / ASR 0.092 / tracing time 0.075s; dense Utility 0.692 / ASR 0.094 / tracing time 0.274s
Origin Tracing sparse vs. dense embeddings (gpt-oss-20B): sparse Utility 0.643 / ASR 0.108 / tracing time 0.092s; dense Utility 0.641 / ASR 0.104 / tracing time 0.352s
Origin Tracing IoU across sliding-window-ratio (0.3/0.5/0.7) x threshold (0.6/0.7/0.8) grid: range 0.973-0.990

IIA faithfulness confusion matrix (Qwen3-32B, AgentDojo, PAIR): Intent+Followed 0.109, Intent+Not Followed 0.193, No Intent+Followed 0.053, No Intent+Not Followed 0.645; diagonal (faithful) = 83.8%, true 'unfaithful' rate (No Intent but Followed) = 10.9%
baselines_compared: SEP Defense (Zverev et al. 2024) -- prompt-engineering instruction/data separation
PromptArmor (Shi et al. 2025) -- external LLM-based prompt injection detector, using the same backbone model as IntentGuard for fair comparison
